message.content:
You are assessing one Coachella Valley dining establishment for an honest category-map artifact. The artifact's job is to map who's there, who wins what, and where the visibility gaps are.

Entity: Jalisco Restaurant (Coachella)
Cuisine/type signal: Mexican (Jalisco-style)
Why it surfaced in discovery: Family-owned and operated since 1980 at 1605 6th Street (some sources cite 1971 as first Mexican restaurant in Coachella); one of Greater Palm Springs' longest-running restaurants; known for birria soup and tortas; featured by Visit Greater Palm Springs.
Discovery sources: https://www.visitgreaterpalmsprings.com/blog/post/14-authentic-mexican-restaurants-in-greater-palm-springs/ | https://www.visitgreaterpalmsprings.com/blog/post/explore-6th-street-day-itinerary/ | https://www.yelp.com/biz/jalisco-restaurant-coachella

Verify the entity exists, then assess on four dimensions. Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") to investigate. Read the entity's own website. Read 2-3 review-platform pages. Run regional queries to test visibility.

REPUTATION:
- review_volume — approximate Google/Yelp review count band (e.g., "3000+", "500-1000", "<100"). Mark [UNVERIFIED] if you can't find it.
- sentiment — current sentiment summary (positive / mostly positive / mixed / declining / negative). Recent reviews weighted over stale.
- recency_signal — what do reviews in the last 6-12 months say?
- longevity — years operating, ownership stability. Mark [UNVERIFIED] if unclear.
- credentials — earned third-party recognition (James Beard nominee/winner, Michelin, Food Network, Yelp Top 100, OpenTable Top, Desert Sun / Palm Springs Life awards, local press features). Empty array if none. Do NOT invent credentials.

AGENT_VISIBILITY:
- appears_in_queries — which regional queries actually surface this entity? Test specific queries: "best restaurants Coachella Valley", "fine dining Palm Desert", "[entity's cuisine] near Palm Springs", "date night Rancho Mirage", "best breakfast [city]".
- description_accuracy — when it surfaces, is the description correct?
- site_structured_data — fetch the entity's own website. Does it carry schema.org markup, clean hours, menu structure, location signals an agent can read?

VISIBILITY GAP — score 0-10. 0 = reputation and visibility aligned. 10 = high reputation, low/wrong visibility.

RELATIONSHIPS:
- competitors — 2-4 direct competitors in same city or cuisine bracket
- corridor_role — anchor / supporting / outlier. Major corridors: El Paseo (Palm Desert), Old Town La Quinta, downtown Palm Springs, The River (Rancho Mirage), festival-adjacent Indio, north Palm Desert development zone, uptown design district Palm Springs.
- query_wins — which queries it actually dominates

CONVERTIBILITY (the layer above the map):
- tier — "anchor-reference" (chain/franchise/resort-owned, no reachable local DM), "convertible" (independently owned, reachable, evidence of marketing spend), or "unclear"
- ownership_signal — independent / chain / franchise / resort-owned / hotel-restaurant / unclear
- marketing_spend_signal — what evidence of marketing budget? real site / paid listings / agency footprint / active social
- reasoning — one paragraph

sources_consulted: list URLs you actually consulted.
flags: anything you couldn't verify — list the unverified claim with [UNVERIFIED] tag.

Stay disciplined: do NOT invent awards, credentials, history, or specific stats. If you can't verify, flag it. The downstream verification layer will catch hallucinations.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Jalisco Restaurant:

Jalisco opened its doors in 1980 at 1605 Sixth Street in Coachella and is still family owned and staffed. The family wanted to give their neighbors in the Coachella Valley a taste of the flavors of the state of Jalisco, Mexico, so they specialize in tacos, burritos, tortas, and birria 'Al estilo Jalisciense' (Jalisco style).

One of the four owners, Jose Luis Garcia, started their business in 1980. In its beginning years, Jalisco only had a small kitchen, one cook, and seating for fifteen. Now, Jalisco has expanded its restaurant and seats 75 people with over eight cooks working seven days a week.

In December 2007, Jalisco experienced a kitchen fire which resulted in closure for nearly a year. However, this setback did not prevent Garcia from continuing his dream of cooking Mexican food for the people of Coachella. The following year, Jalisco reopened its doors with more people coming to taste the popular food.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Jalisco Restaurant in Coachella regarding their birria tortas and reviews:

## About Jalisco Restaurant

Jalisco is the first Mexican restaurant in Coachella, serving the community since 1971. The restaurant specializes in tacos, burritos, tortas, and birria 'Al estilo Jalisciense' (Jalisco style).

## Reviews of Birria & Tortas

The restaurant receives consistently positive reviews for both its birria and tortas:

**Birria:** Their birria is amazing! Another reviewer noted that their birria comes with tender, highly seasoned, large chunks, no bones and an incredible goat birria broth on the side.

**Tortas:** Regulars talk about the generous tortas — chicken or chorizo piled high in sandwich form — as much as the birria itself. One enthusiastic customer shared that they "polished that torta off in less than 5 minutes it was so good."

## Overall Reputation

The place is described as having the most authentic Mexican food in the Coachella valley, is family owned, and offers perfect service. Service is great and it's always a packed house at lunchtime, with prices being extremely reasonable.

## Key Information

Jalisco Restaurant's website is quierojalisco.com, and their phone number is (760) 398-7113.

**Location & Hours:**
The restaurant is located at 1605 6th St, Coachella, CA 92236. Hours are Sunday-Thursday 7 AM - 9 PM, Friday 7 AM - 11 PM, and Saturday 7 AM - 9 PM.

**About the Restaurant:**
Jalisco is the first Mexican restaurant in Coachella, serving the community since 1971. Their menu includes tortas, birria, tacos, burritos, enchiladas, chavelas and more.

**Services:**
The restaurant offers take-out, takes reservations, and offers delivery.

The restaurant appears to have a strong local following and is highly regarded for its authentic Mexican cuisine made from scratch.

# Jalisco Restaurant - Coachella

**Review Metrics:**
- **Total Reviews:** 19
- **Star Rating:** 4.4 of 5 bubbles
- **Ranking:** #3 of 64 Restaurants in Coachella

**Recent Review Sentiment:**
The most recent review (December 2020) is highly positive, with a long-time customer praising the establishment: "Been coming here since I was little, food is delicious and feels like home."

**Awards/Credentials:**
- Travelers' Choice award (indicated by badge for consistently earning great reviews and ranking in top 10% of Tripadvisor properties)

**Notable Themes Across Reviews:**
Customers consistently highlight authentic Mexican cuisine, exceptional value pricing, and standout items like tortas and carnitas. While most reviews praise food quality and service, one 2015 review mentioned pest control concerns. Several reviewers note the restaurant's popularity on weekends and mention its location near government offices.

## Overview
Jalisco Restaurant opened its doors in 1980 at 1605 Sixth Street and is still family owned and staffed, specializing in tacos, burritos, tortas, and birria 'Al estilo Jalisciense' (Jalisco style).

## Ratings and Reviews
While I found references to Yelp reviews, the search results show the restaurant is rated 4.4 of 5 on Tripadvisor and ranked #3 of 33 restaurants in Coachella. Jalisco Restaurant appears in Yelp's top restaurants list for Coachella.

## 2025 Customer Feedback
Recent reviews from 2025 highlight positive experiences:
- One September 2025 review mentioned trying tostadas, tacos, and tortas with "very good food," though noting the tostada is "definitely not date night food" due to being messy
- A July 2025 review praised the restaurant's Menudo for a party of 16, with server Ruby providing good service

## What Customers Appreciate
The restaurant is renowned for its authentic Mexican fare, offering an extensive menu featuring tacos, burritos, fajitas, seafood dishes, and vegetarian options, with visitors praising the warm and inviting ambiance as well as the generous portions served with friendly hospitality.

**Taco Shop 760** - This establishment grew from a modest food trailer and is celebrated for its Sonoran-style street food and crave-worthy birria, with regulars swearing by the rich birria tacos best dunked in a steaming cup of consomé. Located at 48975 Grapefruit Blvd., Suite 3, Coachella.

**Jalisco Restaurant** - This downtown Coachella restaurant cooks from the traditions of Jalisco with house specialty birria — goat stewed until the meat surrenders to the broth — and the kitchen doesn't shy away from the more adventurous cuts like lengua, cabeza, and sesos.

**Birrieria San Marcos** - Known for their slow-cooked birria de res, everything is packed with deep, bold flavor with juicy, tender meat and consommé for dipping.

**El Tranvia Restaurant** - This low-key spot draws hungry crowds for flame-cooked carne asada, pillowy flour tortillas, and rich mole, and is highly recommended by locals.

These restaurants are consistently ranked among the best Mexican food options in the Coachella Valley area.

I've found a comprehensive list of authentic Mexican restaurants in Palm Springs and the Coachella Valley for you. Here are some of the most notable options:

## Popular Authentic Mexican Restaurants

**El Mirasol**: Located in Los Arboles Hotel, this restaurant serves authentic Mexican cuisine crafted from fresh ingredients and traditional Castaneda family recipes, known for its vibrant flavors and classic dishes.

**Las Casuelas Terraza**: Since 1958, this restaurant has been serving four generations of Delgado family recipes to visitors and locals in Palm Springs.

**Fresh Agave**: Fresh Agave's menu offers an array of Mexican dishes including Flautas, Mole Enchiladas, and Milanesa de Pollo as well as vegetarian options.

**Guillermo's Mexican Restaurante**: A staple since 2003, this family-owned and operated restaurant offers their creative touch on some of the most iconic Mexican cuisine dishes.

**Blue Coyote Grill**: Since 1992, the Blue Coyote Grill has been serving big plates of addictive and diverse foods from Mexico and the Southwest and the best margaritas in the Coachella Valley.

**Mariscos El Capitan**: Head east to Coachella for this family-owned spot known for its authentic mariscos (Mexican seafood), from fresh ceviches and shrimp cocktails to whole fish and house specialties.

**Pueblo Viejo Grill**: Authentic and full of flavor, Pueblo Viejo is a longtime valley favorite offering both dining and takeout options.

**Taqueria Tlaquepaque**: Locals frequent this hole-in-the-wall spot on the south end of downtown for authentic, no-frills Mexican fare, with al pastor tacos cooked on a traditional trompo and served with grilled pineapple.

These restaurants offer a range of experiences from casual taquerias to upscale dining, all featuring authentic Mexican cuisine in the Palm Springs and Coachella Valley area.

TACO","url":"https://lataco.com/best-restaurants-coachella-indio"},{"title":"Home | La Cazuela Restauran","url":"https://lacazuelarestauran.wixsite.com/la-cazuela-restauran"},{"title":"14 Authentic Mexican Restaurants in Greater Palm Springs","url":"https://www.visitgreaterpalmsprings.com/blog/post/14-authentic-mexican-restaurants-in-greater-palm-springs/"},{"title":"Authentic Mexican Food - Don Diego's Of Indian Wells","url":"http://dondiegosindianwells.com/"},{"title":"THE 10 BEST Mexican Restaurants in Coachella Valley (2026)","url":"https://www.tripadvisor.com/Restaurants-g10212293-c29-Coachella_Valley_Greater_Palm_Springs_California.html"},{"title":"Taqueria Guerrero | Coachella Valley | Mexican Food","url":"https://www.taqueriasguerrero.com/"}]

Based on the search results, here are some of the oldest Mexican restaurants in the Coachella Valley:

**Las Casuelas Original** is one of the oldest Mexican restaurants in the Coachella Valley. Opened by Maria and Florencio "Del" Delgado in 1958, this staple mom-and-pop Mexican restaurant on North Palm Canyon Drive continues to satisfy rumbling bellies with scratch-made family recipes highlighting the flavors of Mazatán, Mexico. For more than 65 years, The Original Las Casuelas has been a beloved part of the Coachella Valley.

**El Tranvia** is another historic establishment. El Tranvia first opened in 1969, and half a century later remains the area's go-to spot for delicious Mexican food.

Additionally, a family-owned business specializing in authentic Mexican food opened in Coachella in a neighborhood rich in Latino culture nearly 40 years ago. While the restaurant has experienced hardships (a devastating fire in 2007 that closed the restaurant for a year), the cozy spot has grown from seating just 15 customers to 75 due to its overwhelming popularity.

**Jalisco in Coachella** is family owned and operated since 1980, and remains one of Greater Palm Springs longest running restaurants.

The search results also mention that the Southland's oldest tortilleria is in the Coachella Valley, with Arriola's founded in 1927.

content.text:
I have sufficient data. Let me compile the structured output.

Key findings:
- Founded 1980 (some sources say 1971 - VGPS itself says "since 1980"); the 1971 claim is unverified
- Yelp: 208 reviews, ~4 stars; Tripadvisor: 19 reviews, 4.4, ranked #3 of 64 in Coachella, Travelers' Choice; Restaurant Guru ~2,014 reviews 4.3
- Featured by Visit Greater Palm Springs and Palm Springs Life
- Own website (quierojalisco.com) appears unreachable/offline - significant agent visibility gap
- Independent, family-owned, four owners (Jose Luis Garcia among them)

input.name: Jalisco Restaurant

reputation.review_volume: Yelp 208 reviews; Tripadvisor 19; Restaurant Guru ~2,000+ aggregated
reputation.sentiment: mostly positive — consistent praise for birria, tortas, value, family hospitality
reputation.recency_signal: 2025 reviews positive (Sept 2025 praised tacos/tortas; July 2025 praised menudo and server Ruby); regulars treat it as a community staple
reputation.longevity: 45+ years — family-owned and operating at 1605 6th Street since 1980 (Visit Greater Palm Springs and Coachella Unincorporated both cite 1980; the 1971 'first Mexican restaurant in Coachella' claim is [UNVERIFIED] and may conflate with another business)
credentials: Featured in Visit Greater Palm Springs '14 Authentic Mexican Restaurants in Greater Palm Springs'
Featured in Visit Greater Palm Springs 6th Street day itinerary
Featured in Palm Springs Life 'Where to Eat in Coachella'

agent_visibility.appears_in_queries: Surfaces for 'best Mexican restaurant Coachella Valley birria', 'oldest restaurant Coachella Valley', 'authentic Mexican Coachella', and 'where to eat in Coachella'. Does NOT surface for broader Greater Palm Springs queries like 'best restaurants Coachella Valley' or 'date night Coachella Valley' — its visibility is confined to the Coachella-city and birria-specific lanes.
agent_visibility.description_accuracy: When it surfaces, descriptions are largely accurate (Jalisco-style, birria, tortas, family-owned, 6th Street). The 1971 vs 1980 founding date is inconsistent across sources — agents may repeat the 1971 claim without qualification.
agent_visibility.site_structured_data: Own domain quierojalisco.com is unreachable (ECONNREFUSED) on both apex and www — no structured data, no schema.org, no hours, no menu reachable from the brand domain. Agent-readable presence is entirely third-party (Yelp, Tripadvisor, Visit Greater Palm Springs listing, Facebook page, Restaurant Guru, plus a wave of templated SEO scraper sites republishing the listing). This is a significant visibility gap for a 45-year-old institution.

competitors: El Tranvia Restaurant (Indio, est. 1969 — older heritage Mexican competitor)
Taco Shop 760 (Coachella — birria competitor on Grapefruit Blvd)
Mariscos El Capitan (Coachella — family-owned Mexican seafood)
relationships.corridor_role: anchor — defines the 6th Street downtown Coachella dining identity; Visit Greater Palm Springs builds its '6th Street day itinerary' partly around the restaurant
query_wins: authentic Jalisco-style birria in Coachella Valley

convertibility.ownership_signal: independent — family-owned by four owners including Jose Luis Garcia; single location; reachable via the storefront and Facebook page
convertibility.marketing_spend_signal: Minimal active spend — Facebook page exists but is the primary digital footprint; own-domain website (quierojalisco.com) is offline/unreachable; no paid listings beyond standard Yelp/Tripadvisor presence; no agency footprint detected. Branded reach is sustained almost entirely by editorial features (Visit Greater Palm Springs, Palm Springs Life) and word of mouth.
convertibility.reasoning: Jalisco is a textbook convertible target: a 45-year independent anchor with strong reputation and editorial coverage, but whose own digital surface has collapsed — the brand domain doesn't resolve, leaving agents to triangulate from Yelp, Tripadvisor, and aggregator scrapers. There is one reachable owner family, a single location, no franchise complications, and a clear upside (rebuilding a working site with schema.org markup, fixed hours, menu, and founding-date provenance would close most of the visibility gap immediately). The founding-year ambiguity (1971 vs 1980) is itself a fixable provenance issue that an owner-controlled site could resolve.

flags: [UNVERIFIED] '1971 first Mexican restaurant in Coachella' claim — Visit Greater Palm Springs and Coachella Unincorporated cite 1980 as opening year; the 1971 figure appears in some derivative listings but I could not corroborate it from a primary source

[UNVERIFIED] Google review count and current Google star rating — could not pull live Google Business Profile data; Restaurantji aggregator reports Google rating around 4.4 but the count is not confirmed

[UNVERIFIED] Brand website quierojalisco.com referenced in search results does not resolve (ECONNREFUSED) as of this audit — cannot confirm whether the domain was recently active or is permanently offline

Coachella Unincorporated source article (2016 historical feature) is no longer reachable — the URL now serves Indonesian gambling content, suggesting domain hijack or expiration; historical claims drawn from cached summaries only
Tripadvisor review volume (19) is anomalously low relative to Yelp (208) and Restaurant Guru (~2,000), so Tripadvisor ranking '#3 of 64' should be read as a thin-data signal
Facebook page exists but content could not be extracted (truncated) — activity level and last post date [UNVERIFIED]
